author: remy
content:
I have five concerns, ordered by severity. I'll stop before I run out of things to say — the list is long enough.

**1. The human spec review gate is a rubber stamp waiting to happen.**

The design says: 'Human reads the seeder specs, approves proposed mappings or adjusts them, and sets human_action: approve | adjust | skip on each spec.' Fourteen specs, one reviewer, no tooling beyond a text editor.

I've watched this pattern in practice. The 'human review' step sounds meaningful at design time. In execution it becomes: open file, scroll through 40-line YAML, see that proposed_mappings looks reasonable, set human_action: approve, move to the next one. Fourteen times. The reviewer has no external ground truth to check the mappings against during review — the format doc is what the subagent already read, so they'd have to re-read it themselves to verify. Most won't. They'll trust the subagent output.

The design acknowledges this indirectly in the Error Handling section: 'extraction_quality: poor but format doc is good — the spec can be written from the format doc.' But if the subagent's reading of the format doc was wrong or shallow, the spec carries that error forward, gets approved by a fatigued reviewer, and bakes the error into a Go recognizer function. The error then propagates silently into capability YAMLs for months until someone notices a provider's reported capability doesn't match reality.

The review isn't designed to catch wrong mappings — it's designed to feel like oversight while providing very little. The design should either (a) drop the pretense and call this 'author review for obvious errors only,' or (b) add a concrete verification step: run syllago capmon seed immediately after approval and show the human the actual output YAML so they can compare it against the format doc. That makes the review meaningful instead of ceremonial.

**2. The canonical keys table is premature — and once committed, it will resist correction.**

The design commits 13 canonical keys before any inspection of 12 of the 14 providers has run. The keys are defined in the design document and will be committed to docs/spec/skills/capabilities.md as part of the success criteria. Two providers (crush and roo-code) are the only ones with real data; the rest are empty stubs.

This is the classic 'define the schema from one example' mistake. The recognizeSkillsGoStruct function in recognize.go reads Skill.* struct fields and generates frontmatter_* capability keys. That works for the two providers that implement the Agent Skills open standard. When the inspection phase runs on the other 12 providers, it will find things that don't fit. Providers will have capability concepts that don't map cleanly to frontmatter_* keys — location schemes, invocation controls, versioning — and the table will need expansion or revision.

The design has an escape hatch: 'New keys are added to this table when the inspection phase discovers provider-exclusive fields.' But adding is easier than revising. Once a key name is committed to a spec-adjacent file, changing it means touching all the recognizer functions that used it. The path of least resistance becomes adding a new key even when the existing key is named wrong, which gives you a polluted table full of near-synonyms.

Committing the key table before inspection is the wrong sequencing. Run inspection on all 14 providers first, collect the proposed_mappings from all 14 specs, then define the canonical table from actual data. This adds one iteration but produces a table that's grounded in the full provider landscape instead of extrapolated from two providers that happen to share an open standard.

**3. syllago capmon inspect dispatches a subagent to write format docs — this inverts the trust relationship.**

The design says the inspection subagent reads docs/provider-formats/<slug>.md as 'ground truth' and also writes or updates that file if gaps are found. A file cannot be ground truth if it's also written by the tool consuming it.

In practice: a subagent reads a format doc, finds it incomplete, extends it with inferences from the extracted cache, marks the extended version as ground truth for the seeder spec, and writes the proposed_mappings against its own extensions. The human reviewer sees a seeder spec that says extraction_quality: good and proposed_mappings that look reasonable. But the ground truth those mappings came from was generated, not sourced.

For providers where format docs already exist and are human-authored (the majority), this is fine. For the two providers missing docs (crush and pi), the design explicitly says the subagent 'reads source manifest + raw cache content instead' and 'writes new format doc.' This means the crush and pi format docs will be subagent-generated documents that are then treated as authoritative references in subsequent runs. That's a circular trust chain: the extraction informed the format doc, and the format doc validates the extraction.

This should be flagged explicitly as a limitation. The crush and pi seeder specs should carry a note that their format docs are inferred, not sourced. Alternatively, separate the format doc authoring step from the inspection step — make the human write or verify the format doc before inspection runs. The design already has the provider-sources YAML as a manual step (step 1 in the onboarding workflow); the format doc could be the same.

**4. The new provider onboarding workflow installs a permanent bottleneck in the inspect step.**

The documented onboarding sequence is six steps, with syllago capmon inspect as step 3. The inspect subcommand dispatches a Task subagent to read format docs and extracted cache. This means every new provider onboarding requires an LLM call gated on an available subagent, with output that requires human review before proceeding.

For 14 providers this is a one-time cost. For ongoing re-inspection when providers update their docs, it's a recurring cost. The design says steps 2-6 are 'repeatable when the provider updates its docs' — but in practice, provider doc updates will happen at different cadences, and someone has to notice, trigger the re-inspection, and complete the review cycle. That workflow has no automation hook and no signal for 'provider docs may have changed.'

The design would benefit from being explicit about the maintenance model: who triggers re-inspection, how often, and what the signal is. 'Repeatable' is not a maintenance model — it's a statement that the workflow can technically be run again. What makes someone run it?

**5. The recognizer architecture adds a provider parameter to a shared function — this is a breaking change with no migration plan.**

RecognizeContentTypeDotPaths currently has signature: func RecognizeContentTypeDotPaths(fields map[string]FieldValue) map[string]string. The design proposes adding a provider parameter. LoadAndRecognizeCache also gains a provider parameter.

The design says 'The seed command already has --provider and passes it through.' That resolves the seed command call site. But recognize.go is a package-internal function — the question is whether LoadAndRecognizeCache is called from anywhere else in the codebase. In a pipeline like capmon, test files and integration harnesses often call internal functions directly. Adding a required parameter to a function that's called from tests means updating every test call site.

This is the smallest of my five concerns and is straightforwardly fixable, but the design treats it as trivial ('gains a provider parameter') without acknowledging the test impact. The engineers should audit all call sites before starting implementation, not after.

To summarize: concern 1 (rubber stamp review) and concern 3 (circular trust in format doc authoring) are structural problems with the human-in-the-loop design. Concern 2 (premature canonical keys) is a sequencing problem that will cause maintenance debt. Concerns 4 and 5 are smaller but real. The design is implementable but will produce lower-quality output than its success criteria suggest unless concerns 1, 2, and 3 are addressed before implementation planning begins.
